sections.heading: Overview
sections.paragraph: The pharmaceutical landscape is witnessing a significant shift toward structurally complex small molecules, particularly targeted protein degraders and induced-proximity therapeutics, which demand integrated development strategies to ensure clinical success. WuXi AppTec highlights that traditional workflows are insufficient for these modalities, necessitating a unified approach connecting discovery, development, and manufacturing under a single quality system. Dr. Dave Madge, VP of Discovery Services at WuXi AppTec, emphasizes that the field is moving toward a deliberate discovery paradigm where advances in screening and structural biology make processes more predictable. Targeted protein degraders convert transient binding events into catalytic protein removal, introducing complexities like ternary complex formation and linker topology that classical medicinal chemistry rarely addressed. To improve translational success, the industry is adopting technologies such as DNA-encoded libraries, fragment-based screening, and flow chemistry within coordinated systems. This integration allows process decisions made early to carry forward, compressing timelines without sacrificing continuity. WuXi AppTec positions its CRDMO platform as essential for biotech and pharmaceutical innovators navigating these challenges, offering end-to-end services across Asia, Europe, and North America to support breakthrough treatments. The focus remains on shifting from target druggability to target engagement strategy, ensuring that synthetic complexity and analytical performance are optimized from the first gram to commercial supply.

points.text: WuXi AppTec — Targeted Protein Degraders — General Disease Targets — Integrated development support for complex small molecules
WuXi AppTec: Providing integrated CRDMO services to support biotech and pharmaceutical innovators developing targeted protein degraders and covalent agents through end-to-end R&D and manufacturing.

Targeted protein degraders mechanism converts transient binding events into catalytic removal of target proteins, requiring optimization of ternary complex formation and linker topology during discovery.
